content.full_text:
Re
Fnrn PolrrrcAL PRACrrcEs C oMMrssroN
-128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 9581,1-2329
(916) 322--s660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
June 25,2008
Lewis Chilton
2014 Holly Street
Yountville, CA94599
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-093
Dear Mr. Chilton:
This letter responds to yollr reqllest for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")l and is based on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of
fact when it renders advice. (ln re Oglesb.,- (1975) t FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our
advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability,
if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or
Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
Do you, as a town councilmember, have a conflict of interest that would preclurle
you from participating in upcoming votes regarding a planned Community Center
project?
CONCLUSION
Based on the information you provided, you may participate in the governmental
decisions related to the Community Center project.
FACTS
You are a town councilmember for the town of Yountville. As of March l, 2008,
you and your wife operate a delicatessen in Yountville. Your wife owns 75 percent of
the business, operated as a limited liability corporation. while you own the remaining
25 percent. The Deli has a 30 year lease on the retail space in which it operates. Neither
you nor your wife owns the property. The terms of the lease are as follows: the
termination date of the lease is fixed on March l, 2038; subleasing is prohibited: your
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 l(X)0 through 9 l0l,:1. All
statutory ref'erences are kl fhe Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission ale contained in Sections l8ll0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitirrnia
Cirde of Regulations. All regulatilry ret'erences are kr Title 2. Division 6 of the Califirrnia Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-08-093
Page No.2
deli is to operate in the space; there are renewable options every five years for the
duration of the lease.
Since 1998, the Council has been pursuing a project to build a Community Center
on several parcels of property that Yountville owns ("the Site"). The Community Center
project would include various community rooms, a gymnasium, a public library, public
parking, and a sheriffs' substation. Located on the Site are an existing Community Hall
and U.S. Post Office, which leases its space from Yountville; both will receive some
renovations. The Site, as proposed, does not contain retail space for sale or leasing to
private commercial enterprises.
The proposed Community Center Site is in the heart of Yountville's business
district, which is highly concentrated along a one mile segment of Washington Street.
Your deli is located within 500 feet of this site.
The town council will be making decisions relation to the design, constmction,
financing and operation of the Community Center project.
In your letter requesting advice, you provided your own analysis of your question
under Commission regulations including facts indicating construction of the Community
Center would have no financial impact on your lease under the criteria set forth in
Regulation 18705.2(a)(2). You also state that, as to whether construction of the
Community Center will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
business under Regulation 18705(c)(a), "there is not a substantial likelihood . . . that any
of the materiality standards set forth in Regulation 18705(cXa) will be met related to"
decisions regarding the Community Center project.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
Steps One and Two: Are you a public official making, participating in making, or
inlluencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the Yountville Town Cor-rncil. you are a public official under the
Act. (Section 82048.) You will be called Lrpon to consider wherher the Town should
proceed with the Community Center project in its various stages. Therefore, you will be


File No. A-08-093
Page No. 3
making, participating in, making, or otherwise using your official position to influence a
governmental decision.'
step Three: Do you have a potentialty disqualifying conflict of interest?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o 
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, paftner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section g7103(d);
Regulation 1 8703. l(b));
o 
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2):
o 
An economic interest in any sollrce of income, including promised
income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3);
o 
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87 103(e); Regulation 181.03.4);
o 
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including
those of his or her immediate family -- this is the ',personal financial
effects" mle. (Section 87103; Regulation 1g703.5).
Section 82033 defines real property as: "... any leasehold, beneficial or
ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the
jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the publit offi.iot ....,, Becalrse
you and your wife have entered into a 30-year lease of the retail space where you operate
your deli, and that leaseis presumably valued at $2,000 or more, you have an economic
interest in real property.''
When a public otficial who holds an otTice specified in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in
a decision noticed at a public meeting. then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economic interest, as discussed in regulation I 8702.5(b). on the record of the meeting: (2 ) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room fbr the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions. consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests.
special rules found in regularion 18702.5(c) and 1g702.5(d) apply.
I See regulation 18729(b) fbr valuing leasehold interests.


File No. A-08-093
Page No. 4
Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise
operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, parlnership, firm,
business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association." For a business entity
to be considered an economic interest, an official must have an investment of $2,000 or
more or be a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or hold any position of
management. You have a 25 percent ownership interest in the deli, and an investment
over $2,000.4
Step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
"In order to determine if a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision." (Regulation 18704(a).) For govemmental decisions that affect
real property interests, the standards set forth in regulation 18704.2 apply. (Regulation
I8704(a)(2).) For governmental decisions that affect business entities, the standards set
forth in regulation 18704.1 apply. (Regularion 1870a(a)(t).)
Real Propertv:
Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part:
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of
the following apply:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property [that] is
the subject of the governmental decision."
Because the real property in which you have an economic interest is within 500 feet of
the subject of the upcoming governmental decisions, your property interest is directly
involved. [f the real property is directly involved in the governmental decisions, the
materiality standard set forth in Regulation 18705.2(a) applies. (Regulation
18704.2(d)(1).) (See Step 5 below for discussion of the applicability of Regulation
18705.2.)
a You also may have customers that are sources of income to you of g500 or more during the l2
months prior to a decision, based on your pro rata 25 percent share of income to the deli and a one-half
community property share in income to your wife. These would also be economic interests. Because you
have not asked about these sources of income. however, we do not analyze that question or whether you
may be subject to the exception fbr sources of income to retail business entities Jnder Regulation 18707.5


File No. 4-08-093
Page No. 5
Business Entity:
Regulation 18704.1 (a) states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subJect
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person."
For indirectly involved business entities, the materiality standards set forth in
Regulation 18705.1(c) apply. (Regulation 18704.1(b).) (See Step 5 below for discussion
of the applicability of Regulation 18705.1.)
Your facts do not indicate that you will be initiating any proceeding or that you or
your deli is a named party in or the subject of the proceeding involving the Community
Center project. Therefore, your business is indirectly involved. For indirectly involved
business entities, the materiality standards set forth in Regulation 18705.1(c) apply.
(Regulation 18704.1(b).) (See Step 5 below for discussion of the applicability of
Regulation 18705.1.)
Step Five: Materiality Standard
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation I 8700(a).)
Real Property:
For real property leaseholds directly involved in a governmental decision, the
materiality standards in Regulation 18705.2(a)(2) provide:
"(a) Directly involved real property
{...}
"(2) Real property, leaseholds. The financial effect of a
governmental decision on the real property in which an official has a
leasehold interest is presumed to be material. This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
govemmental decision will have any effect on any of the following:


File No. A-08-093
Page No. 6
"(A) The termination date of the lease;
"(B) The amoLtnt of rent paid by the lessee for the leased real
property, either positively or negatively;
"(c) The value of the lessee's right to sublease the real property,
either positively or negatively;
"(D) The legally allowable Llse or the current use of the real
property by the lessee; or
"(E) The Llse or enjoyment of the leased real property by the
lessee."
As stated under the facts above, you have analyzed each of the factors above as applied to
your leasehold interest and the upcoming projects. Under the facts you have p.orid"d,
there do not appear to be any factors involved in the decision that would have any impact
on the lease premises as far as the terms indicated in (A-E) above, and you may,
therefore, be able to rebut the presumption that the decision will have a material effect on
your leased property. Our analysis, however, is limited to the facts as you presented
them.
Business Entity:
For business entities not directly involved in the governmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1 provides for materiality standards that vary according to the financial
size of the business. For relatively small businesses such as your deli (those with net
income of less than $500,000 for the most recent fiscal year), the materiality standards set
forth in Regulation 18705.1(cX4) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)( I )-(3), the
financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
As stated under the facts above, you have concluded that no decisions
regarding the Community Center project would have a material financial effect on
your business as outlined in Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).


File No. A-08-093
Page No. 7
Step Six: Reasonably Foreseeable
An effect Llpon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occlrr. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A finanCial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (ln re Thonter (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good
faith effort to assess the financial effects of the decision by using some reasonable and
objective method of valuation. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-1 13: MoockAdvice
Letter, No. A-01-140 O'Horra Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)
Your analysis of the materiality standard above will determine whether there is a
substantial likelihood, as defined in Regulation 18706(a), that any of the materiality
standards set forth in Regulation 18705.1(cX4) will be met related to decisions regarding
the Community Center project.
In addition, based on your facts, the presumption of materiality can be rebutted
with respect to your leased property. For this reason, no conflict of interest exists in
relation to your real property lease in decisions related to the Community Center project.
Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Exceptions
As you have not offered facts to support either exception, we do not address them.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact mi at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
U$fu^rt,[
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl

Do you, as a town councilmember, have a conflict of interest that would preclurle
you from participating in upcoming votes regarding a planned Community Center
project?

decisions related to the Community Center project.

of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the

materiality standards in Regulation 18705.2(a)(2) provide:
"(a) Directly involved real property
{...}
"(2) Real property, leaseholds. The financial effect of a
governmental decision on the real property in which an official has a
leasehold interest is presumed to be material. This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
govemmental decision will have any effect on any of the following:
"(A) The termination date of the lease;
"(B) The amoLtnt of rent paid by the lessee for the leased real
property, either positively or negatively;
"(c) The value of the lessee's right to sublease the real property,
either positively or negatively;
"(D) The legally allowable Llse or the current use of the real
property by the lessee; or
"(E) The Llse or enjoyment of the leased real property by the
lessee."
As stated under the facts above, you have analyzed each of the factors above as applied to
your leasehold interest and the upcoming projects. Under the facts you have p.orid"d,
there do not appear to be any factors involved in the decision that would have any impact

For business entities not directly involved in the governmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1 provides for materiality standards that vary according to the financial
size of the business. For relatively small businesses such as your deli (those with net
income of less than $500,000 for the most recent fiscal year), the materiality standards set
forth in Regulation 18705.1(cX4) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)( I )-(3), the
financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
As stated under the facts above, you have concluded that no decisions
regarding the Community Center project would have a material financial effect on
your business as outlined in Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).

An effect Llpon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occlrr. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A finanCial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (ln re Thonter (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good
faith effort to assess the financial effects of the decision by using some reasonable and
objective method of valuation. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-1 13: MoockAdvice

Your analysis of the materiality standard above will determine whether there is a
substantial likelihood, as defined in Regulation 18706(a), that any of the materiality

Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Exceptions
As you have not offered facts to support either exception, we do not address them.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do you, as a town councilmember, have a conflict of interest that would preclurle
you from participating in upcoming votes regarding a planned Community Center
project?

CONCLUSION: Based on the information you provided, you may participate in the governmental
decisions related to the Community Center project.
embedding.first_500_words: Re Fnrn PolrrrcAL PRACrrcEs C oMMrssroN -128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 9581,1-2329 (916) 322--s660 o Fax (916) 322-0886 June 25,2008 Lewis Chilton 2014 Holly Street Yountville, CA94599 Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-08-093 Dear Mr. Chilton: This letter responds to yollr reqllest for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")l and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (ln re Oglesb.,- (1975) t FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION Do you, as a town councilmember, have a conflict of interest that would preclurle you from participating in upcoming votes regarding a planned Community Center project? CONCLUSION Based on the information you provided, you may participate in the governmental decisions related to the Community Center project. FACTS You are a town councilmember for the town of Yountville. As of March l, 2008, you and your wife operate a delicatessen in Yountville. Your wife owns 75 percent of the business, operated as a limited liability corporation. while you own the remaining 25 percent. The Deli has a 30 year lease on the retail space in which it operates. Neither you nor your wife owns the property. The terms of the lease are as follows: the termination date of the lease is fixed on March l, 2038; subleasing is prohibited: your ' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 l(X)0 through 9 l0l,:1. All statutory ref'erences are kl fhe Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission ale contained in Sections l8ll0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitirrnia Cirde of Regulations. All regulatilry ret'erences are kr Title 2. Division 6 of the Califirrnia Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-08-093 Page No.2 deli is to operate in the space; there are renewable options every five years for the duration of the lease. Since 1998, the Council has been pursuing a project to build a Community Center on several parcels of property that Yountville owns ("the Site"). The Community Center project would include various community rooms, a gymnasium, a public library, public parking, and a sheriffs' substation. Located on the Site are an existing Community Hall and U.S. Post Office, which leases its space from Yountville; both will receive some renovations. The Site, as proposed, does not contain retail space for sale or leasing to private commercial enterprises. The proposed Community Center Site is in the heart of Yountville's business district, which is highly concentrated along a one mile segment of Washington Street. Your deli is located within 500 feet of this site. The town council will be making decisions relation to the design, constmction, financing and operation of
